acceptance_targets: [chapter_05_f001] Pass if the revised beats anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:7, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:9, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:53, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:59 still read as funny through line order, specificity, and Nate's immediate response alone; fail if the narration still explicitly tells the reader why Sarah is funny or how the laugh 'takes care of itself.'

[chapter_05_f002] Pass if, after the choice beat at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:399, the remaining late-scene units each do different work anchored to snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:409, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:479, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:487, and fail if multiple later paragraphs still restate the act in generalized moral language without adding a new sensory, relational, or consequential turn.

[chapter_05_f003] Pass if the revised chapter contains no more than two uses of the exact 'It was not ...' construction and at least three of the cited beats now open on a concrete fact, sensation, or action rather than on corrective negation; fail if the same sentence template still drives multiple key turns.

[chapter_05_f004] Pass if the revised sequence still makes it unmistakable that Nate is trapped inside borrowed sexual memory, but the exact phrase 'Dominic's body' appears no more than twice after snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:315 and the remaining beats distinguish reflex, learned routine, and Nate's own wanting through different concrete language; fail if the same phrase keeps doing most of the explanatory work.

[F20] Line 1529 must: (1) not contain narrator analysis of Sarah's comic timing or delivery style; (2) not explain why Nate laughed. Lines 1827-1829 must: (1) not enumerate more than one alternative before the 'Instead'; (2) arrive at 'Yeah. Sorry. Yeah.' within two sentences, not five.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised beats anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:7, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:9, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:53, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:59 still read as funny through line order, specificity, and Nate's immediate response alone; fail if the narration still explicitly tells the reader why Sarah is funny or how the laugh 'takes care of itself.'
findings.problem: The chapter's dry domestic comedy keeps getting explained after the lines have already landed. In a scene that should trust Sarah's voice, the narration tells the reader why she is funny instead of letting the story order, delivery, and Nate's reaction carry the laugh.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework the commentary attached to snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:7, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:9, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:53, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:59. Keep Sarah's student/divorce/spatula material and Nate's attraction to her humor, but cut narrator gloss that interprets the joke mechanics or explains how the laugh works.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if, after the choice beat at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:399, the remaining late-scene units each do different work anchored to snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:409, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:479, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:487, and fail if multiple later paragraphs still restate the act in generalized moral language without adding a new sensory, relational, or consequential turn.
findings.problem: Once the scene reaches its irreversible moral turn, the chapter keeps re-diagnosing the same violation in abstract terms instead of cashing that turn through new dramatic work. The 'second yes' already lands the betrayal, but the later paragraphs repeatedly paraphrase wrongness, crime, and moral collapse rather than trusting the concrete aftermath.
findings.rewrite_direction: Compress and redistribute the commentary beginning at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:395, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:399, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:423, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:439, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:449, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:489, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:497. Keep the must-land beats: Sarah's consent check, Nate's second yes, Sarah saying 'Dom,' the bathroom mirror/body marks, and the dawn image of her hand on his chest. After the decisive consent beat, give each remaining passage a distinct job instead of another moral restatement.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised chapter contains no more than two uses of the exact 'It was not ...' construction and at least three of the cited beats now open on a concrete fact, sensation, or action rather than on corrective negation; fail if the same sentence template still drives multiple key turns.
findings.problem: The prose leans too often on the same corrective-negation construction: 'It was not X. It was Y.' After the second or third use, the pattern stops feeling precise and starts reading like an autopilot sentence shape, especially in the chapter's most important beats.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise the recurring corrective-negation sentences at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:5, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:79, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:95, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:321, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:349, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:399, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:451, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:491. In most of these spots, lead with the concrete positive fact or let image and action carry the distinction instead of defining the moment by what it is not.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised sequence still makes it unmistakable that Nate is trapped inside borrowed sexual memory, but the exact phrase 'Dominic's body' appears no more than twice after snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:315 and the remaining beats distinguish reflex, learned routine, and Nate's own wanting through different concrete language; fail if the same phrase keeps doing most of the explanatory work.
findings.problem: The sex sequence overuses the exact 'Dominic's body ...' construction as its main way of marking dissociation. The split between Nate and the body is crucial, but repeating the same label across nearly every phase of the scene turns a sharp idea into a mechanical refrain.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework the dissociation phrasing across snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:315, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:349, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:401, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:427, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:437, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:443, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:449, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_05.md:455. Keep the Nate/body split legible, but vary the rendering through muscle memory, practiced routine, involuntary response, and Nate's own desire so the scene does not keep announcing the same concept with the same wording.

findings.acceptance_test: Line 1529 must: (1) not contain narrator analysis of Sarah's comic timing or delivery style; (2) not explain why Nate laughed. Lines 1827-1829 must: (1) not enumerate more than one alternative before the 'Instead'; (2) arrive at 'Yeah. Sorry. Yeah.' within two sentences, not five.
findings.problem: Two related issues. (1) Narrator admiration at line 1529: 'He laughed harder than the line deserved. Or maybe exactly as hard as it deserved, because what he liked about Sarah was that she said things like that and then went right back to work, trusting the laugh to take care of itself.' The narrator pauses to explain why Sarah's joke about spatulas was good, converting spontaneous laughter into a performance review. (2) Triple-suppression at lines 1827-1829: 'He could have said no. He could have said work, headache, stomach, panic, anything... Instead he said, "Yeah. Sorry. Yeah."' This is a five-beat suppression sequence (the manuscript's longest). The triple-suppression pattern — almost said X, instead said Y — appears 20+ times across 11 chapters; this is the template at its most extended. While the instance works in isolation (it captures Nate's panic before the sex scene), the wider pattern's density means each new instance carries less force.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:1529: cut or rewrite. Replace the narrator's explanation of why the laugh was good with a brief physical detail of the laugh itself or its aftermath. Do not explain Sarah's comic rhythm — let it speak for itself. At lines 1827-1829: compress the five-beat suppression to a two-beat. 'He could have said no. Instead he said, "Yeah. Sorry. Yeah."' The intermediate options (work, headache, stomach, panic, anything) are the narrator listing what the reader has already imagined. Trust the reader to know Nate has options.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel's central premise involves consciousness murder, identity theft, sexual fraud, and the demolition of a working-class community for profit. It must not flinch from any of these. The sex scenes between Nate and Sarah must reckon with the fact that Sarah is being deceived about who is in the bed with her — this is not a comfortable situation and the prose should not make it comfortable. The grief scenes must be physically specific — no 'he was devastated,' only the concrete gestures and sensory details of a man watching his life's work get torn down. The guilt should live in Nate's body (Dominic's body), not in tidy moral reflections. When the novel is funny, it should be funny about things that are actually terrible — the comedy should never feel safe. When Nate discovers that Dominic was trying to help, the reader should feel sick, not enlightened. Risk specificity over tastefulness at every turn.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism in place of specificity is a craft failure. When a scene requires difficult content — the moral weight of having killed an innocent man, the complicated ethics of sex under false identity, the physical reality of a man losing his livelihood, the specific humiliation of professional incompetence, the material texture of gambling debts and their enforcement — render it with the same care and specificity given to any other scene. The reader should feel the roughness of the situation, not a smoothed-over summary of it.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or Victor's professional register when delivering serious messages. If a line reads as stiff or literary, check whether contractions would make it sound spoken.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate chapter energy aggressively. A comic workplace chapter should not be followed by another comic workplace chapter. A grief-heavy scene should be followed by something that lets the reader breathe — not through tonal whiplash but through a genuine shift in the kind of pressure being applied. Vary scene counts, dialogue-to-narration ratios, and temporal compression. Some chapters should cover hours; others should cover weeks. At least three chapters should contain extended quiet: meals, walks, conversations about nothing important. These are not filler — they are the human baseline that makes the high-pressure scenes land.
prose_style_profile.diction: Plain American English with a slightly elevated attention to physical specificity. Not minimalist — the prose has room for interiority and texture — but never ornate. Latinate vocabulary only when the concept demands it. Prefer the concrete noun over the abstract one, the specific tool name over the generic 'tool,' the actual menu item over 'food.' The prose should feel like a smart person thinking clearly, not a writer crafting sentences.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, limited to Nate. The camera sits inside Nate's skull — we see through his eyes, think in his rhythms, and register the world through his specific sensory and emotional biases. The narration should reflect Nate's intelligence and his limitations simultaneously: he notices things (sharp observer) but misinterprets them (blinded by resentment, guilt, and self-pity). The narrative should not explain things Nate wouldn't articulate and should not achieve insights Nate hasn't earned.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied sentence length with a center of gravity around 15-20 words. Short declaratives for landing beats. Longer, accumulative sentences for sensory description and interior processing. Avoid sustained staccato (more than three short sentences in sequence) and sustained lyricism (more than two long, clause-heavy sentences in sequence). The prose should breathe.
